primer.markdown:
# Effects as types — stateful code, still verified

At Level 5 the type system gains a whole new axis. Alongside `int`, `list a`, and refinements, every computation now carries an **effect label** — `Tot` for total, `Dv` for divergent, `ST` for stateful, `GTot` for erasable ghost code. The effect *is part of the type*. F* can talk about state, exceptions, and divergence inside the same logic that proved `clamp` and `fibonacci`, and it can *mix* them through effect polymorphism and lifting.

The canonical L4/L5 example in the fstar-framework is `swap`, written in the `ST` (state) effect with an explicit Hoare triple:

```fstar
let swap (#a: Type) (r1 r2: ref a) : ST unit
  (requires (fun h -> True))
  (ensures (fun h0 _ h1 ->
    sel h1 r1 == sel h0 r2 &&
    sel h1 r2 == sel h0 r1 &&
    modifies (only r1 ++ only r2) h0 h1))
=
  let tmp = !r1 in
  r1 := !r2;
  r2 := tmp
```

Three new things are loud in that signature. **Requires** names the precondition on the incoming heap `h`. **Ensures** names the postcondition as a relation between pre-heap `h0`, return value, and post-heap `h1`. And **modifies** names a *frame* — the set of references that can change. F* requires you to prove that *nothing outside the frame is touched*, which is how effect polymorphism composes: if `f : ST _ _ _ (modifies S0)` and `g : ST _ _ _ (modifies S1)`, then sequencing them gives a combined `modifies (S0 ++ S1)`, and the rest of the heap is provably untouched.

The skeleton extrapolates. `ST` is one effect in a lattice — `Tot ⊂ Ghost ⊂ ML ⊂ Dv`, with `ST` sitting orthogonally over the heap. Write your algorithm once over an effect variable; instantiate it for `Tot` when you want a pure extraction, for `ST` when you need real memory. Same code, different effect, same proofs.
primer.key_insight: At L5 the effect is part of the type. `ST unit (requires ...) (ensures ...)` is a Hoare triple embedded in F*'s type system, and the `modifies` clause is the frame condition that makes effect polymorphism compositional.

visual.alt: A diagram of the `swap` Hoare triple. On the left, the pre-heap `h0` with references `r1 -> A`, `r2 -> B`, and `r3 -> C` (outside the frame). An arrow labeled `swap r1 r2` runs to the right side, where the post-heap `h1` shows `r1 -> B`, `r2 -> A`, and `r3 -> C` unchanged. A red dashed rectangle labeled `frame: {r1, r2}` encloses the two affected refs in both heaps; the note reads `modifies (only r1 ++ only r2)`. Above: a small effect-lattice diagram showing `Tot ⊂ Ghost ⊂ ML`, with `ST` branching off to carry the heap.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner inspects the annotated `swap` signature and toggles the `modifies (only r1 ++ only r2)` clause. With it present, the simulator reports `ST unit — verified (frame respected)`. Remove it (replace with `modifies no_refs`) and a third ref `r3` in the example heap is reported as an unproven obligation: `could not prove r3 unchanged`. Success fires when the learner has seen both states.

artifact.excerpt:
let swap (#a: Type) (r1 r2: ref a) : ST unit
  (requires (fun h -> True))
  (ensures (fun h0 _ h1 ->
    sel h1 r1 == sel h0 r2 &&
    sel h1 r2 == sel h0 r1 &&
    modifies (only r1 ++ only r2) h0 h1))
=
  let tmp = !r1 in
  r1 := !r2;
  r2 := tmp

questions.prompt: In the `swap` signature, `sel h1 r1 == sel h0 r2` appears in the `ensures` clause. What do `h0` and `h1` refer to, and what is `sel`?
questions.explanation: `h0` is the **pre-heap** (state before the call), `h1` is the **post-heap** (state after), and `sel h r` reads the value stored at reference `r` in heap `h`. The ensures clause is therefore the relational statement 'the value of r1 after the call equals the value r2 held before the call' — the core correctness of a swap.

questions.prompt: The `modifies (only r1 ++ only r2) h0 h1` clause is the **frame condition**. Why does F* require it?
questions.explanation: Effect polymorphism is only compositional if you can talk about what a computation *does not* touch. The frame condition names the set of references `{r1, r2}` that `swap` may write to, and F* then proves every other reference in the heap is unchanged — so when you sequence `swap r1 r2; do_something_with r3`, the solver knows `r3`'s value survived intact.

questions.prompt: The framework document places `ST` in a lattice of effects. What is the practical consequence of `Tot ⊂ ST` in that lattice?
questions.explanation: Every total (pure) computation can be **lifted** into the stateful effect for free — the heap is simply unchanged. That is what lets you write a library function once in `Tot` and call it safely from `ST` code. The subsumption is one direction only: you cannot silently demote an `ST` computation to `Tot`, because that would lose the heap reasoning.
